section: Strengthening Mechanisms in Low Density Fe–26Mn–xAl–1C Steels

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the abstract, introduction, experimental procedures, and the initial part of results and discussion for Fe–26Mn–xAl–1C steels (3Al, 6Al, 10Al, 12Al). It provides full processing route (induction melting, hot rolling at 1050°C with 85% reduction to 3 mm, solution treatment at 1100°C/1h/WQ). Optical and TEM microstructures are described: fully austenitic for 3–10Al, ferrite (7%) appears in 12Al; grain sizes are measured with and without twin boundaries. κ-carbide superlattice spots appear in 10Al (weak) and 12Al (strong). Yield strengths from a previous study are given: 321, 378, 470, 952 MPa. Strengthening contributions are quantified: solid solution coefficient (17.54 MPa/wt%Al, base 275.57 MPa), Hall–Petch parameters (σ0=250.70 MPa, kG=683.07 MPa·μm^1/2), and individual contributions for 12Al (solid solution ~203, grain refinement ~61, ferrite ~50, κ-carbide ~362 MPa). Microhardness values for austenite and ferrite are reported. The chunk also discusses κ-carbide precipitation during quenching for Al>10 wt% and the difference in strain hardening behavior (TWIP in low Al, glide plane softening in high Al). Key figures: Fig. 1 (optical & TEM), Fig. 2 (stress-strain curves and Hall–Petch), Fig. 3 (strengthening contributions diagram). Subsequent chunks may detail further discussion on deformation mechanisms, additional TEM/APT analysis, or conclusions.
processing_route: hot rolled at initial 1050°C to 3 mm thick, 85% reduction
solution treated at 1100°C for 1 h, water quenched
microstructure: Grain size 80 μm (3Al-10Al, without twins), 40 μm (12Al without twins), 39 μm (10Al with twins), 16 μm (12Al with twins)
κ-carbides identified by superlattice spots in 10Al (weak) and 12Al (strong)
properties: Yield strength: 3Al 321 MPa, 6Al 378 MPa, 10Al 470 MPa, 12Al 952 MPa
Microhardness: 6Al austenite 244.4 kgf/mm², 12Al matrix 399.1 kgf/mm², 12Al ferrite 332.6 kgf/mm²
Strain hardening present in 3Al,6Al; absent in 12Al
mechanisms: Precipitation hardening by κ-carbides (dominant in 12Al, 361.93 MPa)
TWIP effect in low Al steels
Glide plane softening in high Al, high carbide fraction steels
figures_or_tables: Figure 2: engineering stress-strain curves and Hall–Petch plot

Material systems: Fe-26Mn-xAl-1C (wt%) with x = 2.59 (3Al), 5.84 (6Al), 9.92 (10Al), 11.58 (12Al); phases: austenite (γ), ferrite (α), κ-carbide (Fe,Mn)3AlCx
Sample aliases: 3Al; 6Al; 10Al; 12Al

Mechanisms: Solid solution hardening by Al; Grain boundary strengthening (Hall–Petch); Precipitation hardening by κ-carbides (dominant in 12Al, 361.93 MPa); Ferrite strengthening (50 MPa for 7% fraction); TWIP effect in low Al steels; Glide plane softening in high Al, high carbide fraction steels
Figures or tables: Figure 1: optical micrographs (a–d) and TEM (e,f); Figure 2: engineering stress-strain curves and Hall–Petch plot; Figure 3: strengthening contributions diagram

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the final discussion on strain hardening and the conclusions. For low Al steels (3Al, 6Al), TWIP effect causes strain hardening. Increasing Al raises stacking fault energy, making TWIP unlikely; instead, microbands or refined slip bands dominate deformation. In 12Al steel, a large fraction of carbides induces glide plane softening, so no significant strain hardening occurs. In the conclusions, Al up to 12 wt% enables grain refinement, triplex microstructure, and solid solution strengthening, while precipitation strengthening from nanosized precipitates is the dominant mechanism, being 1.78 times the solid solution effect. This chunk closes the paper with final strengthening assessment and deformation mechanism differences. Subsequent chunks may not exist, but the main conclusions are captured here.
properties: strain hardening present in 3Al and 6Al; absent in 12Al
mechanisms: microbands or dynamic slip band refinement (higher Al)
precipitation strengthening dominant (1.78 times solid solution)
